md: Intuitively, a **continuous** function is one whose graph you can draw without lifting your pen. Formally, continuity is defined in terms of limits — and most theorems in calculus assume the functions involved are continuous.
## 1. The Three Conditions
A function $f$ is **continuous at $a$** if all three are true:
**1.** $f(a)$ is defined (i.e., $a$ is in the domain).
**2.** $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} f(x)$ exists (the two-sided limit).
**3.** $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$ (the limit equals the function value).
If any condition fails, $f$ has a **discontinuity** at $a$.
## 2. Types of Discontinuity
**Removable (hole):** the limit exists but doesn't equal $f(a)$ — or $f(a)$ doesn't exist. We could 'fix' it by redefining $f(a)$.
Example: $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2}$ at $x = 2$. The limit is 4 (we computed it earlier); but the function isn't defined there. Hole at $(2, 4)$.
**Jump:** the left and right limits both exist but are different.
Example: a step function. $\lim_{x \to 0^-} = 0$, $\lim_{x \to 0^+} = 1$ — function jumps.
**Infinite (vertical asymptote):** the function diverges to $\pm\infty$.
Example: $f(x) = 1/x$ at $x = 0$.

blocks.title: Continuous on an interval
blocks.body: $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ if it's continuous at every point in the interval. (For endpoints, use one-sided limits.) Polynomials, exponentials, sines, cosines are continuous everywhere. Rational functions are continuous everywhere except where their denominator is 0.

md: ## 3. Why Continuity Matters
Continuous functions behave nicely:
**Direct substitution works** for limits: $\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$.
They satisfy the **Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)**.
They have a **maximum and minimum** on any closed bounded interval (Extreme Value Theorem).
They can be **integrated** (and the integral has nice properties).
Most theorems in differential and integral calculus require continuity as a hypothesis.
## 4. The Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)

blocks.title: Intermediate Value Theorem
blocks.body: If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $N$ is any value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then there exists at least one $c$ in $(a, b)$ with $f(c) = N$.

md: **Plain English:** a continuous curve from height $f(a)$ to height $f(b)$ must pass through every height in between. It can't skip a value.
**A useful corollary:** if $f$ is continuous and $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ have *opposite signs*, then $f$ has at least one **root** in $(a, b)$. This is the basis of root-finding by bisection.
**Example.** Show that $f(x) = x^3 - x - 1$ has a root between 1 and 2.
$f(1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 < 0$. $f(2) = 8 - 2 - 1 = 5 > 0$. $f$ continuous (polynomial). By IVT, $f$ has a root somewhere in $(1, 2)$.
### Worked example — analyze continuity
$f(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & \text{if } x < 1 \\ 3 - x & \text{if } x \geq 1 \end{cases}$
Is $f$ continuous at $x = 1$?
**Check 2:** $\lim_{x \to 1^-} f(x) = 1^2 = 1$. $\lim_{x \to 1^+} f(x) = 3 - 1 = 2$. **Left ≠ right** — limit doesn't exist.
Condition 2 fails. **Jump discontinuity at $x = 1$**.
